code_verification: receiver method exists on the receiver type
static methods are called statically and member methods are called on valid instances
assignment target is compatible with the method return type
argument count, argument type, and overload shape match the public API
enum owner and enum value both exist
property constants use the correct owner and value type
signal callback signatures match the public signal typedef
code blocks do not rely on inferred namespaces or overloads from naming alone
draft_requirements: Write enough practical detail for application developers to use the feature in a dali-ui app.
Ground natural-language behavior claims in the actual public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when the context pack is not enough.
Before writing examples, inspect the referenced public headers and relevant sample files when needed.
Prefer examples copied or adapted from existing samples when they match the feature; otherwise keep examples small and verify every API use.
After drafting, review the completed Markdown line by line against repository evidence and fix inaccurate explanations or code before returning.
repair_requirements: Repair only the problematic spans and the smallest surrounding context needed; do not rewrite the whole document.
For each listed issue, use the repository evidence and context pack to verify the corrected wording or code before returning.
If an issue reveals the same API misuse nearby, fix the nearby equivalent misuse too; do not make unrelated style rewrites.
After repair, re-check the edited lines against public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when needed.

rules: Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.
Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.
Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples.

usage_patterns.intent: Create and configure the primary object using public dali-ui APIs.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Prefer typed constructors and setters when available.

usage_patterns.intent: React to user or resource events through public signals.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Use signal connection APIs only with their owning trait or class.

rendered_prompt:
You are reviewing an existing DALi dali-ui application developer guide draft.
This is Phase 2.3.5: source-grounded prose review.

Primary goal:
- Improve natural-language accuracy and guide flow using repository evidence.
- Preserve the document's useful structure and examples whenever possible.
- Do not perform a full rewrite.
- Keep the revised user-facing guide in en only.
- Remove Korean, Japanese, Chinese, or other non-English prose from the revised guide.

Feature target:
- Feature: Focus Manager (focus-manager)
- Category: input-interaction
- Guide profile: dali-ui-app
- Audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

Review rules:
- Review every natural-language paragraph line by line against public headers, implementation files, samples, and UTCs when needed.
- Accuracy is the first priority. Flow is the second priority.
- Edit the smallest unit needed: word, sentence, paragraph, or section-local addition/removal.
- If a word-level change is enough, change only the word.
- If a sentence-level change is enough, change only the sentence.
- If an entire paragraph is based on a wrong premise, revise, add, or remove that paragraph.
- Preserve code blocks when possible.
- If prose can be corrected to match an already accurate code block, prefer changing prose.
- If the code block itself makes the prose inaccurate or breaks the guide flow, rewrite only the smallest necessary part of that code block and verify it against public headers/source.
- Do not invent APIs.
- Do not add generic filler just to make the document longer.
- Do not change section structure unless prose_review.allow_section_restructure is true or the current prose is clearly misleading.
- Prefer localized edits when prose_review.localized_edits_only is true.
- Preserve code blocks especially when prose_review.preserve_code_blocks is true.
- Prefer Dali::Ui::View, typed setters, fluent chaining, and view-tree idioms for app-facing guidance.
- Treat dali-core and dali-adaptor APIs as reference/context unless the feature explicitly requires them.

Original Markdown draft:
---
title: Focus Manager
sidebar_label: Focus Manager
category: input-interaction
---

# Focus Manager

`Dali::Ui::FocusManager` controls keyboard focus for `Dali::Ui::View` objects in a dali-ui application.

## Table of Contents

- [Getting the Application Focus Manager](#getting-the-application-focus-manager)
- [Requesting Focus for Views and Containers](#requesting-focus-for-views-and-containers)
- [Moving Focus](#moving-focus)
- [Using Focus Groups](#using-focus-groups)
- [Customizing the Focus Indicator](#customizing-the-focus-indicator)
- [Reacting to Focus Changes](#reacting-to-focus-changes)
- [Clearing and Preserving Focus](#clearing-and-preserving-focus)
- [Inspecting the Last Focus Change Device](#inspecting-the-last-focus-change-device)

## Getting the Application Focus Manager

Use the singleton `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get()` when application code needs to request, move, inspect, or clear focus. Store the returned `Dali::Ui::FocusManager` handle where your controller configures focus behavior.

```cpp
void ConfigureFocus(Dali::Ui::View initialView)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  const bool focused = focusManager.RequestFocus(initialView);
  if(focused)
  {
    Dali::Ui::View currentFocus = focusManager.GetCurrentFocusView();
  }
}
```

`Dali::Ui::FocusManager` is the app-facing focus controller. The focused object exposed by the manager is a `Dali::Ui::View`, so application focus code should keep working with view handles rather than raw scene actors.

## Requesting Focus for Views and Containers

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::RequestFocus()` for normal application focus requests. It accepts a `Dali::Ui::View` and returns `true` when focus was applied.

```cpp
bool FocusPrimaryAction(Dali::Ui::View primaryAction)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  if(focusManager.RequestFocus(primaryAction))
  {
    return focusManager.GetCurrentFocusView() == primaryAction;
  }

  return false;
}
```

`Dali::Ui::FocusManager::SetCurrentFocusView()` is stricter: it tries to set focus directly to the supplied `Dali::Ui::View`. Use it when you intentionally want the exact view to become focused.

```cpp
bool RestoreExactFocus(Dali::Ui::View previouslyFocusedView)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  return focusManager.SetCurrentFocusView(previouslyFocusedView);
}
```

For container-style UI, `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::RequestFocus()` is usually the better entry point because it can resolve focus through the view hierarchy. If you pass a container view, the request can land on an eligible focusable descendant; if no descendant accepts focus and the container itself can accept focus, the container view can become focused.

```cpp
bool OpenPanelAndFocusFirstItem(Dali::Ui::View panel)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  const bool accepted = focusManager.RequestFocus(panel);
  Dali::Ui::View focusedView = focusManager.GetCurrentFocusView();

  return accepted && focusedView;
}
```

## Moving Focus

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::MoveFocus()` when your application needs to trigger directional navigation in code. The method takes a `Dali::Ui::FocusDirection` and returns whether the move succeeded.

```cpp
bool MoveSelectionRight()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  return focusManager.MoveFocus(Dali::Ui::FocusDirection::RIGHT);
}

bool MoveSelectionDown()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  return focusManager.MoveFocus(Dali::Ui::FocusDirection::DOWN);
}
```

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::MoveFocusBackward()` when you want to return to the previously focused view.

```cpp
void ReturnToPreviousFocus()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  focusManager.MoveFocusBackward();
}
```

Forward and backward traversal can be triggered explicitly with `Dali::Ui::FocusDirection::FORWARD` and `Dali::Ui::FocusDirection::BACKWARD`.

```cpp
bool MoveToNextFormField()
{
  return Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get().MoveFocus(Dali::Ui::FocusDirection::FORWARD);
}

bool MoveToPreviousFormField()
{
  return Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get().MoveFocus(Dali::Ui::FocusDirection::BACKWARD);
}
```

## Using Focus Groups

A focus group is a containment boundary for focus navigation. Mark a container `Dali::Ui::View` with `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::SetAsFocusGroup()` when focus movement should stay inside that view subtree, such as a dialog, drawer, or local component panel.

```cpp
void EnableDialogFocusGroup(Dali::Ui::View dialogRoot, Dali::Ui::View firstControl)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  focusManager.SetAsFocusGroup(dialogRoot, true);
  focusManager.RequestFocus(firstControl);
}
```

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::IsFocusGroup()` to inspect whether a view is currently marked as a focus group.

```cpp
bool IsContainedPanel(Dali::Ui::View panelRoot)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  return focusManager.IsFocusGroup(panelRoot);
}
```

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::GetFocusGroup()` to find the nearest focus group that contains a view. It returns an empty `Dali::Ui::View` handle when the view does not belong to any focus group.

```cpp
bool IsInsideFocusGroup(Dali::Ui::View view)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  Dali::Ui::View group = focusManager.GetFocusGroup(view);

  return static_cast<bool>(group);
}
```

Disable the boundary by calling `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::SetAsFocusGroup()` with `false`.

```cpp
void DisableDialogFocusGroup(Dali::Ui::View dialogRoot)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get().SetAsFocusGroup(dialogRoot, false);
}
```

## Customizing the Focus Indicator

`Dali::Ui::FocusManager` owns the focus indicator used to highlight the currently focused view. Replace the indicator with `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::SetFocusIndicatorActor()`, passing a `Dali::Ui::View` that represents your indicator.

```cpp
void InstallFocusIndicator(Dali::Ui::View focusIndicator)
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  focusManager.SetFocusIndicatorActor(focusIndicator);

  Dali::Ui::View activeIndicator = focusManager.GetFocusIndicatorView();
}
```

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::GetFocusIndicatorView()` when you need to inspect the currently configured indicator view.

```cpp
Dali::Ui::View CurrentFocusIndicator()
{
  return Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get().GetFocusIndicatorView();
}
```

## Reacting to Focus Changes

Connect to `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::FocusChangedSignal()` to observe global focus transitions. The callback receives the previously focused `Dali::Ui::View` and the newly focused `Dali::Ui::View`.

```cpp
class FocusObserver : public Dali::ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Start()
  {
    Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get().FocusChangedSignal().Connect(
      this,
      &FocusObserver::OnFocusChanged);
  }

private:
  void OnFocusChanged(Dali::Ui::View previousFocus, Dali::Ui::View currentFocus)
  {
    mPreviousFocus = previousFocus;
    mCurrentFocus = currentFocus;
  }

  Dali::Ui::View mPreviousFocus;
  Dali::Ui::View mCurrentFocus;
};
```

`Dali::Ui::FocusManager::GetCurrentFocusView()` is useful inside or outside the signal callback when you need to query the current state directly.

```cpp
Dali::Ui::View QueryCurrentFocus()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  return focusManager.GetCurrentFocusView();
}
```

## Clearing and Preserving Focus

Call `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::ClearFocus()` when the current view should no longer own focus, such as when closing a focused panel.

```cpp
void CloseFocusedPanel()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  focusManager.ClearFocus();
}
```

By default, focus is cleared when the window loses focus. Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::SetClearFocusOnWindowFocusLost()` to change that policy and `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::GetClearFocusOnWindowFocusLost()` to read it back.

```cpp
void PreserveFocusAcrossWindowFocusLoss()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  focusManager.SetClearFocusOnWindowFocusLost(false);

  const bool clearsOnWindowFocusLost =
    focusManager.GetClearFocusOnWindowFocusLost();
}
```

Re-enable the default behavior when your view state should reset after the application window loses focus.

```cpp
void RestoreDefaultWindowFocusPolicy()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get().SetClearFocusOnWindowFocusLost(true);
}
```

## Inspecting the Last Focus Change Device

Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::GetLastFocusChangeDevice()` to inspect what kind of input caused the most recent focus change. Use `Dali::Ui::FocusManager::GetLastFocusChangeDeviceName()` when you also need the device name.

```cpp
bool LastFocusChangeWasProgrammatic()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();

  return focusManager.GetLastFocusChangeDevice() ==
         Dali::Ui::FocusDevice::PROGRAMMATIC;
}
```

```cpp
const Dali::String& LastFocusChangeDeviceName()
{
  Dali::Ui::FocusManager focusManager = Dali::Ui::FocusManager::Get();
  return focusManager.GetLastFocusChangeDeviceName();
}
```

This can help application code distinguish between focus changes initiated by an API call and focus changes initiated by user input, while keeping the focus state itself managed through `Dali::Ui::FocusManager`.
